short.headline: Heart disease kills 3.1 million Indians a year, more than the next two causes combined
short.dek: Non-communicable diseases have overtaken infections as the top killers. Cardiovascular disease, cancers, and respiratory illnesses lead, but injuries and suicide hit working-age Indians hardest.
short.body: In 2023, cardiovascular disease alone killed 3.12 million Indians, according to the Global Burden of Disease. That is more than cancers (1.06 million) and chronic respiratory disease (1.25 million) combined. Diabetes killed 5.95 lakh, while infectious diseases like tuberculosis (3.35 lakh) and diarrhoea (4.62 lakh) have fallen sharply from their 1980 levels. Neonatal disorders claimed 2.98 lakh, road injuries 2.47 lakh, and suicide 2 lakh. The story is one of epidemiological transition: India now dies the way richer, older countries do, of chronic diseases. But beneath the all-ages chart, the picture shifts. For babies, birth complications dominate. For schoolchildren, drowning. For young adults, injuries and suicide. The best India-specific check, the SRS Cause of Death survey, finds cardiovascular disease at 32.1% of all deaths, with non-communicable diseases overall at 60.1%. These numbers are estimates, not certified counts, but they paint a clear direction.

macha.heading: Okay, but what does this actually mean, na?
macha.body: Bottom line: the thing most likely to kill an Indian today is a heart attack or stroke. It used to be diarrhoea, TB, or childbirth. That swap happened because India got vaccines, antibiotics, cleaner water, and hospital deliveries. But as people started living longer, they ran into diseases of ageing, high BP, sugar, cholesterol. Now even young adults are dying of heart disease, and road crashes and suicide take a shocking toll in the prime years. The government’s own survey of death causes backs this up: one in three deaths is from heart or circulatory trouble. The fine print? Most Indians still die at home, without a doctor’s note, so these numbers are smart guesses, not exact headcounts. Still, the big picture is solid: India’s fight has shifted from germs to lifestyles and safety.
macha.soWhat: India’s biggest killer is heart disease, by a wide margin, and the top risk factors are now what you eat, how you move, and the air you breathe.

article.title: What kills Indians?
article.standfirst: A breakdown of India’s leading causes of death reveals a nation in the grip of non-communicable diseases, with heart disease far ahead. But age and sex tell starkly different stories, from drowning in childhood to suicide in middle age.
article.bodyMarkdown:
## What kills the most Indians, 2023?

Heart disease, and it is not close. Cardiovascular disease, heart attacks and strokes, killed 3.12 million Indians in 2023, according to the Global Burden of Disease study. That is more than the next two causes combined: chronic respiratory disease (1.25 million) and cancers (1.06 million). Diabetes caused 5.95 lakh deaths, and diarrhoeal diseases 4.62 lakh. Even lower respiratory infections, which topped the charts a generation ago, are now at 4.12 lakh.

This pyramid of death is the signature of an epidemiological transition. India has moved from dying of infections, diarrhoea, TB, pneumonia, to dying of non-communicable diseases, or NCDs. These are chronic, non-infectious conditions driven partly by ageing, partly by lifestyle and environment. The catch: these are modelled estimates, not certified death counts. Most Indians still die at home, without a doctor to record why. The GBD fills those gaps with statistical models, making it the best comparative picture, but not a tally of death certificates.

## What kills babies (under 5)?

For the youngest, death looks nothing like the all-ages chart. The biggest killer is being born too soon: preterm birth complications killed 1.57 lakh children under five in 2023. Lower respiratory infections were next (1.35 lakh), then birth asphyxia and trauma (85,600). Congenital defects (80,900) and diarrhoeal diseases (35,300) follow, with neonatal sepsis at 31,600.

The pattern is clear, birth and the first month are the most dangerous period. These are not chronic lifestyle diseases; they are conditions of early infancy, closely tied to maternal health and care around delivery. The decline in diarrhoeal deaths from 8.2 lakh in 1980 to 35,300 today is one of India’s great public health victories, driven by oral rehydration salts and better sanitation.

## What kills children (5 to 14)?

Once a child passes their fifth birthday, the threats shift dramatically. In 2023, drowning was the leading cause of death among 5- to 14-year-olds, killing 7,800 children. Cancers came second (7,300), followed by diarrhoeal diseases (6,800), road injuries (6,000), lower respiratory infections (5,800), and cardiovascular diseases (4,100).

Drowning is a stark but often overlooked danger, open water bodies, lack of swimming skills, and weak supervision turn a routine bath or swim into a tragedy. The numbers are small relative to adult mortality, but each is a child lost to a cause that is largely preventable. The presence of cancers on this list is a reminder that childhood malignancies, though rare, are real and require specialized care.

## What kills young adults (15 to 49)?

Working-age Indians die in ways the all-ages ranking hides. Cardiovascular disease still leads, with 3 lakh deaths, but road injuries (1.48 lakh) and suicide (1.46 lakh) are right behind, making them the third and fourth biggest killers in this age band. Cancers killed 1.87 lakh, digestive diseases 99,500, and tuberculosis 80,700.

This is the tragedy of dying young. Road crashes and self-harm are not diseases but injuries, largely preventable and often concentrated in the prime earning years. The suicide figure (2 lakh nationally, but 1.46 lakh in the 15-49 group) is a red flag. India’s police records report a lower 1.7 lakh suicides overall, but GBD models a higher number, and stigma ensures undercounting everywhere. TB’s persistence among young adults is another reminder that infectious threats have not vanished, only receded.

## What kills the middle-aged (50 to 69)?

In middle age, non-communicable diseases take over completely. Cardiovascular disease killed 1.22 million people aged 50-69 in 2023, a staggering number. Cancers were next at 5 lakh, followed by chronic respiratory disease (3.69 lakh), diabetes (2.46 lakh), tuberculosis (1.34 lakh), and digestive diseases (1.32 lakh).

The dominance of cardiovascular is so complete that it accounts for more deaths than the next three causes combined. This is the stage when risk factors like high blood pressure, smoking, and air pollution exact their toll. TB, though lower than in younger adults, still claims over a lakh lives, a reminder that some infectious diseases linger into older age cohorts.

## What kills the elderly (70 and older)?

Among Indians who live past 70, cardiovascular disease is overwhelming. It killed 1.59 million elderly people in 2023, more than the next three causes put together. Chronic respiratory disease (8.32 lakh) was a distant second, followed by cancers (3.55 lakh), diabetes (3.1 lakh), and diarrhoeal diseases (2.97 lakh). Dementia (1.21 lakh) makes its first appearance in the top six.

In one sense, this is a mark of success: people are surviving childhood and middle age only to encounter the diseases of very old age. But it also signals a new challenge: managing chronic multimorbidity and conditions like dementia that are poorly captured in India’s healthcare and death data. The diarrhoeal numbers among the elderly show that even in age, infections remain dangerous when immunity wanes.

## How do men and women die differently?

India’s own death survey, the SRS Cause of Death, offers a rare look at sex differences. In 2022-24, non-communicable diseases caused 62.3% of male deaths and 56.9% of female deaths. But the sharpest gap is injuries: 12.6% of male deaths were due to injuries versus only 7.5% of female deaths. Conversely, ill-defined causes, where the reason remains unclear, were far more common among women (12.9%) than men (7.4%).

Men’s higher injury share reflects greater exposure to road traffic, workplace hazards, and violence. The higher ill-defined share for women suggests that their causes of death go less often recorded or are not probed as deeply by verbal autopsy. This is not a biological difference but a social one, hinting at gaps in how India investigates why its women die.

## How did heart disease deaths triple since 1980?

In 1980, cardiovascular disease killed 9.73 lakh Indians. By 2023, that number had more than tripled to 3.12 million. The line chart rises sharply, especially after 2000. This is not because Indians suddenly developed weaker hearts. It is because the population has grown, people live longer, and an older population has more heart attacks.

Critically, this measures absolute deaths, not the age-adjusted risk. If you accounted for ageing, the risk might look flatter. But even so, the raw count matters: health systems must treat 3.12 million heart attacks and strokes each year, triple the load of 1980. Urbanisation, dietary changes, physical inactivity, and rising diabetes all play a role, but the chart alone cannot prove causation, it shows the result.

## What are the four non-communicable killers driving India’s death toll?

Cardiovascular, cancers, chronic respiratory, and diabetes, these four NCDs have been rising in parallel. Cardiovascular deaths leaped from 9.73 lakh in 1980 to 3.12 million in 2023. Chronic respiratory rose from 3.58 lakh to 1.25 million. Cancers increased from 2.52 lakh to 1.06 million. Diabetes, once rare, surged from 74,700 to 5.95 lakh.

The slopes differ. Cardiovascular is the steepest by far, but diabetes saw the most dramatic relative rise, an eightfold increase. Chronic respiratory and cancers climbed more slowly. Together, these four likely account for the majority of Indian deaths today, and their collective weight is still rising. The lines are not merely a curiosity; they show where India’s preventive, diagnostic, and treatment efforts are increasingly needed.

## What does India’s own death survey find?

The SRS Cause of Death survey (2022-24) is India’s own nationally representative cause data, using verbal autopsy on a sample of deaths. It finds cardiovascular disease at 32.1% of all deaths, a number that remarkably echoes the GBD’s message. Cancers are at 7%, respiratory diseases 6%, digestive 5.9%, respiratory infections 5.7%, and fever of unknown origin 4.1%. Unintentional injuries account for 4.1%, diabetes 3.6%, genito-urinary 3.4%, and TB 2.6%.

Broadly, non-communicable diseases caused 60.1% of deaths, communicable, maternal and nutritional diseases 19.7%, and injuries 10.5%. Ill-defined causes were 9.7%. This survey counts the rural and at-home deaths that hospital records miss, making it the important reality check. Unlike the GBD, it is not a long time-series, it’s a single snapshot, but it confirms the heart is at the root of India’s mortality.

## What was the COVID scar and how did it recover?

COVID-19 killed an estimated 52,400 Indians in 2023, according to GBD. But the line chart shows a spike in 2020 and 2021, the pandemic years, before falling back. The GBD peak is lower than some excess-death studies, which suggested far larger tolls, but every source agrees the spike was sharp and then receded.

The point is not to argue about the absolute count, COVID mortality is genuinely uncertain, but to note that COVID was a shock, not a permanent shift. By 2023, its direct death toll had already dropped below many chronic diseases. The bigger story is the disruption COVID caused to other diseases and healthcare, but that is not visible in these cause-specific death numbers.

## Did India’s own death rate spike during COVID?

The crude death rate from India’s Sample Registration System tells a similar story. In 2018, it was 6.2 per 1,000 population. During COVID, it shot up, SRS bulletins reported 7.5 in 2021, and then fell back to 6.4 by 2023.

This is not an age-adjusted measure, so part of the bump is due to COVID deaths and part due to an ageing population. But the jump and decline are unmistakable: the pandemic temporarily pushed mortality higher across the country. The recovery to 6.4 suggests India’s underlying death rate is still close to its pre-pandemic level, though population ageing will keep gradual pressure upward.

## How have infectious disease deaths fallen?

The other half of the epidemiological transition is the retreat of infectious killers. Lower respiratory infections fell from 6.75 lakh deaths in 1980 to 4.12 lakh in 2023. Diarrhoeal diseases crashed from 1.61 million, the biggest killer then, to 4.62 lakh today. Tuberculosis declined from 6.79 lakh to 3.35 lakh. HIV/AIDS, which emerged after 1980, peaked around 2005 and is now at 47,400. Malaria dropped from 1.22 lakh to 15,800.

These declines are the result of immunization, antibiotics, oral rehydration, TB control programmes, insecticide-treated nets, and better water and sanitation. They are among India’s most significant health achievements. But TB remains substantial at over 3 lakh deaths, India still has a quarter of the world’s TB burden, and diarrhoeal diseases still kill nearly half a million, largely among children and the elderly. The job is not finished.

## How many Indians die from injuries and suicide?

Injuries kill about 5.4 lakh Indians each year. Road injuries lead with 2.47 lakh deaths in 2023, up from 80,500 in 1980. Suicide is next at 2 lakh, up from 68,600 forty years ago. Drowning has declined to 49,900, and interpersonal violence is at 44,300.

The injury burden is concentrated among the young and working-age, as the age-ladder charts showed. Suicide is widely believed to be undercounted even in the GBD; India’s police-based National Crime Records Bureau reports about 1.7 lakh suicides. Road deaths have risen with motorization, despite improvements in helmet use and road design. Drowning’s decline is welcome but still a leading cause in childhood.

## How dramatically has maternal mortality fallen?

Maternal mortality, women dying from pregnancy or childbirth complications, has seen the steepest fall on this page. In 1985, India’s maternal mortality ratio was 658 per 100,000 live births, meaning one woman died every 152 births. By 2023, it had dropped to 80 per 100,000, a near 90% decline.

This is a triumph of institutional deliveries, skilled birth attendance, emergency obstetric care, and the Janani Suraksha Yojana. The WHO/UN estimate for 2023 is 80, with an uncertainty range of 73-87. A blip occurred in 2021, when the ratio rose to 155 due to COVID-related service disruptions, but the long trend is unmistakably downward. India is now close to the global target of 70 by 2030.

## Is infant mortality still falling?

The infant mortality rate (IMR) from the SRS declined from 32 per 1,000 live births in 2018 to 25 in 2023. It measures the probability of a child dying before their first birthday. This improvement, even over just six years, reflects better neonatal care, vaccination, and maternal health.

India’s IMR is still higher than many developing countries, but the trend is solid. The SRS is the official source here, not the modelled GBD, so these numbers carry more weight for policy. The fall in IMR is one reason the overall death rate has not risen faster despite population ageing.

## How many total deaths occur each year?

Total deaths in India have been rising, from 9.13 million in 2000 to an estimated 10.6 million by 2030, according to UN projections. In 2000, 4.8 million deaths were male and 4.3 million female. By 2030, the projection is 5.8 million male and 4.8 million female.

This growth is not because people are dying at higher rates, the crude death rate has been fairly flat, but because the population is larger and, importantly, older. An older population produces more deaths even if each age group’s risk is falling. The male excess reflects higher male mortality at most ages. The denominator matters: when you see 3.12 million heart disease deaths, remember that is out of about 10 million total deaths each year.

pullQuotes.quote: Cardiovascular disease killed 3.12 million Indians in 2023, more than the next two causes combined.
Suicide and road injuries are the third and fourth biggest killers of working-age Indians.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: Think of it like a slow, silent engine problem in your body that develops over time, not something you catch from someone else. Heart disease, diabetes, cancer, and chronic lung disease are the big ones. They often come from a mix of genetics, what you eat, how active you are, and the air you breathe.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: NCDs are now the main killers in India, marking a shift from the era when infections dominated. The charts show cardiovascular, cancers, chronic respiratory, and diabetes driving most deaths.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: Imagine a country’s health history in two chapters: first, people die young from infections like TB or diarrhoea; later, as living standards rise, they survive into old age and start dying from heart disease and cancer. That flip is the epidemiological transition. It is why India’s death charts today look more like Europe or Japan than they did in 1950.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: Every chart on this page reflects this transition, the fall of infectious diseases and the rise of NCDs. Understanding it helps you see why the top killer is now cardiovascular, not diarrhoeal disease.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: Since India doesn’t have a full death registration system where every cause is certified by a doctor, researchers use statistical models to estimate what killed people. The GBD, run by the University of Washington, pulls together hospital data, surveys, and other clues to estimate deaths for every cause. It is the most comprehensive global comparison, but it is a model, not a direct count of death certificates.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: Most cause-of-death numbers on this page come from the GBD. They are the best we have for trends and comparisons, but we should remember they are estimates.

glossaryBlocks.term: Sample Registration System (SRS) Cause of Death
glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: This is India’s own answer to the cause-of-death question. The SRS, which tracks births and deaths in a sample of villages and wards, sends trained interviewers to ask families about the symptoms before a death. That is called verbal autopsy. It captures at-home and rural deaths, unlike hospital records alone.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: The SRS Cause of Death survey (2022-24) is the only nationally representative cause data for India. It shows cardiovascular at 32.1% and confirms the NCD dominance, serving as a reality check on modelled GBD estimates.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: This is simply how many people die in a year for every 1,000 people alive, without adjusting for their age. So if a country has many old people, the crude rate can be high even if healthcare is good. It is like counting how many vehicles pass a checkpoint without knowing if they are bikes or trucks, a busy road with many light vehicles can fool you.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: We use the SRS crude death rate to see the overall mortality level and how COVID caused a temporary spike. But we caution that population ageing pushes it up over time.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: When someone dies at home without a doctor, trained interviewers ask relatives about what happened, fever, cough, pain, etc. That information is reviewed by experts to assign a probable cause. It is not as precise as a hospital diagnosis, but it is the only way to get a cause for deaths that happen outside hospitals, which is most Indian deaths.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: The SRS Cause of Death survey relies on verbal autopsy, making it the most inclusive India-specific source. It explains why ‘fever of unknown origin’ and ‘ill-defined’ causes still claim a noticeable share.

dataSelectionAudit.reason: Essential cause-specific deaths are present, but some key temporal points like the COVID-19 peak in 2021 and the SRS crude death rate for that year are not in lockedNumbers, though they appear in the planned charts. The audit from the plan is adopted.

Clear ranking for a small number of causes, important for policy focus.
Small counts; bars make the top cause (drowning) stand out.

Bars highlight the overwhelming cardiovascular share.

Single-round survey; bars show share distribution.

chartExplainers.title: What kills the most Indians, 2023
chartExplainers.takeaway: Cardiovascular disease is the biggest killer, at 3.12 million deaths, more than chronic respiratory and cancers combined.
chartExplainers.detail: This bar chart ranks the top 12 causes of death from the Global Burden of Disease model for 2023. Cardiovascular disease, including heart attacks and strokes, sits at the top with 3.12 million deaths. Chronic respiratory disease (1.25 million) and cancers (1.06 million) are next. Then come diabetes (5.95 lakh), diarrhoeal diseases (4.62 lakh), lower respiratory infections (4.12 lakh), digestive diseases (3.47 lakh), tuberculosis (3.35 lakh), neonatal disorders (2.98 lakh), road injuries (2.47 lakh), suicide (2 lakh), and chronic kidney disease (1.21 lakh). The chart immediately shows that non-communicable diseases dominate, and the top three NCDs account for the bulk of all deaths. These are modelled estimates, not certified counts, because most Indian deaths happen at home without a doctor.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It directly answers the page question with the most comprehensive, comparable data available, making the hierarchy of causes plain at a glance.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Each horizontal bar represents GBD-estimated deaths for a cause. The longer the bar, the more deaths. Read left to right; the number at the end of the bar is the total deaths.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Don't mistake these for official certified death counts. They are modelled and include adjustments for under-registration.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Use large labels and sparse ticks; consider a two-tone colour scheme to differentiate NCDs and infections.

chartExplainers.title: What kills babies (under 5)
chartExplainers.takeaway: Preterm birth complications are the top killer, causing 1.57 lakh deaths, followed by pneumonia-like infections.
chartExplainers.detail: For children under five, the leading cause in 2023 was preterm birth complications, with 1.57 lakh deaths. Lower respiratory infections (pneumonia) killed 1.35 lakh, birth asphyxia and trauma 85,600, congenital defects 80,900, diarrhoeal diseases 35,300, and neonatal sepsis 31,600. The chart makes clear that birth-related conditions dominate, unlike the all-ages chart. The sharp drop from earlier decades is dramatic: in 1980, diarrhoea killed 8.23 lakh children under five. This improvement is largely due to oral rehydration and sanitation.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It shows how the causes of death are completely different for the youngest age group, highlighting the need for perinatal and neonatal care.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Horizontal bars ordered by size, with preterm births the longest. The numbers are specific to under-5s; compare the relative lengths.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: The graph does not show trends; the decline from past decades is mentioned in the text but not drawn on the chart.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Keep bars broad with clear labels; a soft colour for all bars since they are all early-life conditions.

chartExplainers.title: What kills children (5 to 14)
chartExplainers.takeaway: Drowning is the leading cause, killing 7,800 children, just ahead of cancers.
chartExplainers.detail: In the 5 to 14 age group, drowning took 7,800 lives in 2023, making it the top cause. Cancers killed 7,300, diarrhoeal diseases 6,800, road injuries 6,000, lower respiratory infections 5,800, and cardiovascular diseases 4,100. The shift from birth complications to injuries is stark. Drowning often happens in rivers, ponds, and wells, especially in rural areas. The numbers are small relative to adults, but these are potentially avoidable deaths.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It reveals a frequently overlooked cause, drowning, and shows that accidents overtake infections after early childhood.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Short bars, but the ranking is clear. Pay attention to the sudden appearance of drowning and road injuries compared to the under-5 chart.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Don't overlook this chart because the numbers are small; the causes here are critical for child safety policy.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Larger font for the small numbers; maybe annotate the drowning bar with 'leading cause'.

chartExplainers.title: What kills young adults (15 to 49)
chartExplainers.takeaway: Heart disease still leads, but road injuries (1.48 lakh) and suicide (1.46 lakh) are the close third and fourth killers.
chartExplainers.detail: This chart is the revelation hidden by the all-ages ranking. Cardiovascular disease killed 3.03 lakh young adults, cancers 1.87 lakh, but then road injuries (1.48 lakh) and suicide (1.46 lakh) follow closely. Digestive diseases caused 99,500 deaths and tuberculosis 80,700. The injury burden in this productive age group is enormous, nearly 3 lakh deaths from just road crashes and self-harm. Suicide is likely undercounted even in GBD; India's police records report around 1.7 lakh suicides overall.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It uncovers the heavy toll of injuries and suicide among working-age Indians, a pattern invisible in the top-line cause list.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Watch how road injuries and suicide jump up the ranking compared to the broader chart. The bars show absolute numbers, not rates.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Don't interpret the closeness of suicide and road deaths as precision; both have wide uncertainty ranges.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Emphasise the injury bars with a distinct colour to make them stand out.

chartExplainers.title: What kills the middle-aged (50 to 69)
chartExplainers.takeaway: Cardiovascular disease dominates, with 1.22 million deaths, more than the next three causes combined.
chartExplainers.detail: In the 50-69 age band, the NCD takeover is complete. Cardiovascular disease killed 1.22 million, cancers 5 lakh, chronic respiratory disease 3.69 lakh, diabetes 2.46 lakh, tuberculosis 1.34 lakh, and digestive diseases 1.32 lakh. The sheer magnitude of cardiovascular deaths is staggering: it accounts for more than half of all deaths in this group. This is the peak age for heart attacks and strokes, driven by high blood pressure, smoking, and diabetes.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It demonstrates why cardiovascular disease is the number-one national killer, middle age is where it hits hardest in absolute numbers.
chartExplainers.howToRead: The first bar is dramatically longer than the rest. The ranking is less surprising, but the cumulative NCD weight is the story.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: The chart shows absolute deaths; the age-specific rate would show cardiovascular risk rising with age, but the absolute numbers reflect the large cohort size plus risk.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Rotate labels for long cause names; keep the cardiovascular bar a strong colour.

chartExplainers.title: What kills the elderly (70 and older)
chartExplainers.takeaway: Cardiovascular disease caused 1.59 million deaths in the over-70s, and dementia appears in the top six for the first time.
chartExplainers.detail: Among Indians over 70, cardiovascular disease is unchallenged, with 1.59 million deaths. Chronic respiratory disease followed at 8.32 lakh, then cancers at 3.55 lakh, diabetes at 3.1 lakh, diarrhoeal diseases at 2.97 lakh, and dementias at 1.21 lakh. Dementia’s appearance signals a growing challenge as India ages. Diarrhoeal deaths in the elderly remind us that infections still prey on fragile immune systems.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It completes the age-ladder, showing that NCDs are nearly the entire picture in old age, and introduces emerging threats like dementia.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Notice the dominance of cardiovascular, its bar is about twice the size of the second. Dementia’s inclusion is a sign of an ageing population.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Dementia deaths are likely underestimated because diagnosis is rare and often not recorded in verbal autopsies.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Highlight the dementia bar with a callout, since it is a new and rising cause.

chartExplainers.title: How men and women die differently
chartExplainers.takeaway: Injuries cause 12.6% of male deaths but only 7.5% of female deaths, while women have far more ill-defined causes (12.9% vs 7.4%).
chartExplainers.detail: Using the SRS Cause of Death survey, this chart compares the broad cause categories between sexes. Non-communicable diseases are high for both, 62.3% for men, 56.9% for women. But the injuries gap is stark: 12.6% male vs 7.5% female. Conversely, communicable diseases are slightly higher for women (22.6% vs 17.7%), and ill-defined causes are almost double for women (12.9% vs 7.4%). The higher ill-defined share suggests that women’s deaths are less likely to be medically investigated or accurately reported through verbal autopsy.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It highlights sex disparities that are not biological but reflect social and reporting differences, important for targeting prevention and improving data.
chartExplainers.howToRead: The chart shows paired bars for each cause category, with male and female side by side. Focus on the injury and ill-defined gaps.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Don't interpret the smaller NCD share for women as lower risk; it may partly be because more female deaths are ill-defined.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Use side-by-side bars with clear labels; a legend on top.

chartExplainers.title: The rise of heart disease, 1980 to 2023
chartExplainers.takeaway: Cardiovascular deaths more than tripled, from 0.97 million in 1980 to 3.12 million in 2023.
chartExplainers.detail: This line chart plots the absolute number of cardiovascular deaths each year from 1980 to 2023. It shows a relentless upward curve, steepening after 2000. In 1980, there were 9.73 lakh deaths. By 2000, around 1.5 million, and by 2023, 3.12 million. Part of this rise is driven by population growth, more people means more potential heart patients, but ageing is the bigger factor. As Indians live longer, the pool of older adults vulnerable to cardiovascular disease expands.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It displays the scale of the epidemic over four decades, underlining why heart disease is now the central health challenge.
chartExplainers.howToRead: The x-axis runs from 1980 to 2023, the y-axis from about 0.8 million to 3.2 million. Follow the line steadily upward.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: This is not an age-standardized rate, so the rise partly reflects demographic change. The actual risk for a given age may have changed less.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Line chart with marked decades; label the latest value prominently.

chartExplainers.title: The four non-communicable killers
chartExplainers.takeaway: All four major NCDs rose sharply, but cardiovascular stands out with 3.12 million deaths, while diabetes saw the fastest relative growth.
chartExplainers.detail: Four coloured lines show the annual deaths from cardiovascular, cancers, chronic respiratory, and diabetes from 1980 to 2023. Cardiovascular leads by far, climbing from 0.97 million to 3.12 million. Chronic respiratory rose from 3.58 lakh to 1.25 million. Cancers increased from 2.52 lakh to 1.06 million. Diabetes exploded from just 74,700 to 5.95 lakh. The slopes differ: cardiovascular is steepest, but diabetes shows an eightfold increase.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It presents the collective rise of the NCDs that now drive most Indian mortality, allowing visual comparison of their trajectories.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Look for the line that is highest and rising fastest (cardiovascular). Diabetes is the thin line that shoots up from near zero.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: The chart measures absolute deaths, not rates; some rise is due to population growth and ageing, especially for chronic respiratory and cancers.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Use distinct colours and a legend. Keep lines thin to reduce clutter on a small screen.

chartExplainers.title: What India's own death survey finds, 2022-24
chartExplainers.takeaway: Cardiovascular disease is 32.1% of all deaths, with NCDs overall at 60.1%, confirming the GBD picture from a nationally representative sample.
chartExplainers.detail: This bar chart shows the share of each cause among all deaths as per the SRS Cause of Death survey 2022-24. Cardiovascular leads at 32.1%, followed by cancers (7%), respiratory diseases (6%), digestive (5.9%), respiratory infections (5.7%), fever of unknown origin (4.1%), unintentional injuries (4.1%), diabetes (3.6%), genito-urinary (3.4%), and TB (2.6%). The broad breakdown: NCDs 60.1%, communicable, maternal & nutritional 19.7%, injuries 10.5%, ill-defined 9.7%. Unlike the GBD, this is based on verbal autopsies from a representative sample of deaths, including rural and home deaths.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It is the strongest Indian validation of the GBD patterns, using the country's own survey, and grounds the story in local data.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Bars represent percentage of all deaths. The longest bar is cardiovascular. Note the presence of 'fever of unknown origin' and 'ill-defined' as significant categories.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: This is a single survey round, not a trend. It shows the current picture, not change over time.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Group bars by broad category (NCD, communicable, injury, ill-defined) with colour bands.

chartExplainers.title: The COVID scar, and the recovery
chartExplainers.takeaway: COVID deaths spiked in 2020-21 and then fell sharply to 52,400 in 2023, showing the pandemic was a shock, not a permanent new killer.
chartExplainers.detail: The line chart for COVID-19 deaths from the GBD starts at zero in 1980 and stays there until 2020, when it shoots up to a peak in 2021, then drops to 52,400 by 2023. The peak value is not given in the locked numbers, but the chart clearly shows a spike. GBD’s estimates are lower than some excess-mortality studies, but the shape, surge and retreat, is consistent. By 2023, COVID had fallen below many established causes like TB and diarrhoeal diseases.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It captures the pandemic's mortality impact and shows that COVID has receded as a leading cause, important context for interpreting other trends.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Look for the sharp mountain peak around 2020-21, then a drop. The y-axis may be in thousands.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: The absolute numbers are uncertain, so focus on the shape rather than the exact height of the peak.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: A simple line with the spike annotated; large axis labels.

chartExplainers.title: India's own death rate: spike, then recovery
chartExplainers.takeaway: The crude death rate jumped to about 7.5 in 2021 before falling back to 6.4 per 1,000 in 2023, showing a temporary pandemic bump.
chartExplainers.detail: This chart plots the crude death rate from India’s Sample Registration System, covering 2018 to 2023. The rate was 6.2 in 2018, spiked to an estimated 7.5 in 2021 (per SRS bulletins), and then dropped to 6.4 by 2023. The spike is clearly COVID-driven. The recovery to near pre-pandemic levels suggests that the underlying death rate is relatively stable, though population ageing will gradually push it up over the longer term.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It uses India’s own official death registration to illustrate the pandemic’s temporary impact on mortality.
chartExplainers.howToRead: The y-axis is deaths per 1,000 population. Note the 2021 peak well above the 2018 and 2023 values.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: The 2021 value is not in the locked numbers but is part of the SRS series; the chart should include it. The rate is not age-adjusted, so ageing can mask real improvements.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Mark the 2021 point with a label; keep the line smooth.

chartExplainers.title: The retreat of infectious disease
chartExplainers.takeaway: Diarrhoeal deaths plummeted from 1.61 million to 4.62 lakh, and TB fell from 6.79 lakh to 3.35 lakh, marking huge gains.
chartExplainers.detail: Five lines track lower respiratory infections, diarrhoeal diseases, TB, HIV/AIDS, and malaria from 1980 to 2023. Diarrhoeal diseases show the most dramatic decline: from 1.6 million in 1980 to 4.62 lakh in 2023. Lower respiratory infections fell from 6.75 lakh to 4.12 lakh. TB dropped from 6.79 lakh to 3.35 lakh. HIV/AIDS rose after 1985, peaked around 2005, and fell to 47,400. Malaria crashed from 1.22 lakh to 15,800.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It is the essential counterpoint to the NCD rise, showing the success of public health in reducing infectious deaths.
chartExplainers.howToRead: All lines mostly go down. Diarrhoeal and TB show the largest absolute drops. HIV and malaria are now very low.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: The y-axis scale matters; if logarithmic, the declines look less dramatic. The chart shows absolute numbers, so the big drop in diarrhoeal is partly because it started so high.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Use distinct colours; maybe add a shaded band indicating the pre- and post-2000 era.

chartExplainers.title: Death by injury and suicide
chartExplainers.takeaway: Road deaths rose to 2.47 lakh, suicide reached 2 lakh, while drowning declined.
chartExplainers.detail: This multi-line chart displays deaths from road injuries, suicide, drowning, and interpersonal violence from 1980 to 2023. Road injuries climb from 80,500 to 2.47 lakh. Suicide rises from 68,600 to 2 lakh. Drowning falls from 89,000 to 49,900. Interpersonal violence increases slightly from 34,900 to 44,300. The increase in road deaths is linked to motorization. The suicide rise is concerning and may reflect both increased reporting and underlying distress.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It brings together the leading injury causes, showing that they have not fallen like infectious diseases, and for road and suicide, have risen.
chartExplainers.howToRead: The top two lines are rising, the bottom two are flatter. Road injuries are the highest by 2023.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Suicide numbers are likely underestimated in GBD and the real toll could be higher.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Line chart with only four series; use distinctive dashes or colours.

chartExplainers.title: Maternal mortality: the steepest fall
chartExplainers.takeaway: The maternal mortality ratio dropped from 658 to 80 per 100,000 live births between 1985 and 2023, a near 90% decline.
chartExplainers.detail: This line chart plots the WHO/UN maternal mortality ratio for India. It starts at 658 in 1985, declines steadily to 80 by 2023. There is a temporary spike to 155 in 2021 due to COVID-19 disruptions. The uncertainty range (73-87 in 2023) is narrow. This trend reflects a massive expansion of institutional deliveries and skilled birth attendance in India.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It is perhaps the most dramatic public health success story on this page, worth highlighting as a counterpoint to the rise of NCDs.
chartExplainers.howToRead: The y-axis is maternal deaths per 100,000 live births. The line descends steeply from a high starting point. Note the 2021 bump.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: The 2021 rise was a pandemic anomaly, not a reversal of the long-term trend.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Annotate the 1985 and 2023 values; keep the COVID spike labeled.

chartExplainers.title: Fewer babies dying
chartExplainers.takeaway: Infant mortality rate fell from 32 to 25 per 1,000 live births between 2018 and 2023.
chartExplainers.detail: The SRS infant mortality rate for India shows a decline from 32 in 2018 to 25 in 2023. The line chart covers six years and shows a steady downward movement. IMR is a sensitive indicator of overall health system performance, reflecting antenatal care, skilled birth attendance, and postnatal interventions.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It provides the official Indian data on child survival, complementing the age-ladder charts, and shows continued improvement.
chartExplainers.howToRead: The y-axis is infant deaths per 1,000 live births. The line slopes downward. A drop of 7 points in 6 years is significant.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: This is the national average; states like Kerala have much lower rates, while poorer states are higher.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: A simple line with years on x-axis, IMR on y-axis; label the start and end points.

chartExplainers.title: Total deaths each year
chartExplainers.takeaway: Total deaths are projected to rise from 9.13 million in 2000 to 10.6 million by 2030, driven by population growth and ageing.
chartExplainers.detail: This line chart from UN World Population Prospects shows total annual deaths in India, with separate lines for both sexes, males, and females. In 2000, total deaths were 9.13 million (4.81 million male, 4.32 million female). By 2030, they are projected to reach 10.6 million (5.79 million male, 4.81 million female). The rise is steady. The male line is consistently higher. The increase is primarily due to an aging population, the death rate has been relatively flat.
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: It provides the denominator for all other cause-specific charts and shows the demographic pressure on the health system.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Three lines: total (solid), male (dashed), female (dotted). The y-axis is in millions. They all trend upward.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: These are projections for future years and depend on assumptions about fertility and mortality; actual numbers may differ.
chartExplainers.mobileNote: Keep lines distinct; label the 2030 projection point.

sourceNotes: IHME Global Burden of Disease 2023: modelled estimates for cause-specific deaths, 1980–2023.
Sample Registration System (SRS) 2023: India's official vital registration sample, providing crude death rate and infant mortality.
SRS Cause of Death 2022-24: Nationally representative cause distribution using verbal autopsy.
WHO/UN Maternal Mortality Estimation Inter-agency Group (MMEIG): maternal mortality ratio estimates.
UN World Population Prospects 2022: total death projections.
caveats: GBD death counts are modelled statistical estimates and not certified death counts. They incorporate adjustments for under-registration and cause-of-death redistribution.
The SRS Cause of Death survey is a single round (2022-24) and does not yet provide a time series.
COVID-19 death estimates vary widely; the GBD values are lower than some excess-death studies. The true toll remains uncertain.
Suicide is widely underreported in India due to stigma and legal factors. GBD estimates attempt to correct for this but may still be underestimates.
The crude death rate is not age-adjusted, so its rise over time partly reflects population ageing rather than worsening mortality.
Maternal mortality ratio from WHO includes 2021 spike due to COVID-19 service disruptions, but the long-term trend is clearly downward.
Dementia deaths are likely underestimated due to low diagnosis rates and lack of awareness.

editorialPlanDraft.answerThesis: Heart disease is the leading cause of death in India, killing around 3.12 million people in 2023, more than the next two causes combined, as the country has shifted from infectious to non-communicable diseases.
requiredConcepts: The epidemiological transition from communicable to non-communicable diseases.
Non-communicable diseases (NCDs) include cardiovascular disease, cancers, chronic respiratory disease, and diabetes.
Absolute death counts vs rates: rising counts can reflect population growth and ageing, not necessarily higher individual risk.
Crude death rate is deaths per 1,000 population and is not age-adjusted.
GBD estimates are modelled and not certified death counts.
SRS Cause of Death (SRS-CoD) is India's own nationally representative survey using verbal autopsy.
MCCD is the Medical Certification of Cause of Death, covering only ~22% of deaths, skewed urban/institutional.
SRS (Sample Registration System) provides official birth and death rates.
Cause of death varies strongly by age: infants die from birth complications, schoolchildren from drowning, working-age adults from heart disease and injuries, elderly from NCDs.
Men die more from injuries, women have more ill-defined causes.

chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: Underreporting of suicide means the actual number may be higher.

chartPlan.title: Maternal mortality: the steepest fall
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Demonstrates a major public health success.
chartPlan.howToRead: A line from 658 per 100,000 live births (1985) to 80 (2023), a near 90% decline.
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: The 2021 rise to 155 was due to COVID; trend remains downward.

chartPlan.title: Fewer babies dying
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Shows the improvement in infant survival.
chartPlan.howToRead: Infant mortality rate fell from 32 to 25 per 1,000 live births (2018-2023).
chartPlan.mistakeToAvoid: This is the official SRS rate; GBD estimates may differ.

chartPlan.title: Total deaths each year
chartPlan.whyShowThis: Provides the denominator for all cause-specific charts.
chartPlan.howToRead: A line chart showing total deaths rising from 9.1 million (2000) to 10.6 million (2030 projection).

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: Also known as chronic diseases, NCDs are not passed from person to person. They develop over time due to genetics, lifestyle, and environmental factors. Examples include heart disease, cancer, diabetes, and chronic lung disease.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: The page shows that NCDs have become the leading causes of death in India, marking a shift from infectious diseases.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: As a country develops, the pattern of disease shifts: people stop dying mainly from infections and start dying from chronic, non-infectious diseases. This is known as the epidemiological transition.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: Understanding this concept helps explain why heart disease and cancer are now the top killers in India, a pattern once seen only in wealthy nations.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: The Global Burden of Disease study is a research project that estimates death and disability from hundreds of diseases and injuries worldwide. For India, GBD uses statistical models to fill gaps where official death registration is incomplete.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: Many of the cause-specific death counts on this page come from GBD models, not from death certificates. They offer the best available picture but are estimates.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: The SRS is India's long-running survey system that tracks births and deaths in a representative sample of households. It is the source of official life expectancy and death rate data for the country.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: The SRS provides India's own national death rate and the latest cause-of-death data (SRS-CoD), offering a important check on modelled GBD estimates.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: The crude death rate is the number of deaths in a year per 1,000 people. It does not account for the age structure of the population, so an older population can have a higher crude death rate even if age-specific risks are stable.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: We use the crude death rate to see the overall mortality level, but we caution that a rise can partly reflect population ageing.

glossaryPlan.plainMeaning: When a death occurs at home without a doctor, trained interviewers ask family members about the symptoms and circumstances leading to death. This information is then reviewed to assign a probable cause. It's less precise than medical certification but captures deaths that would otherwise go unreported.
glossaryPlan.whyItMattersHere: India's SRS Cause of Death survey uses verbal autopsy, making it the only nationally representative source of cause-of-death data for the country.

plannedCharts.title: What kills the most Indians, 2023
plannedCharts.subtitle: IHME Global Burden of Disease 2023 · estimated deaths, all ages, both sexes
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The direct answer to the page question, ranked. Heart disease dwarfs everything: at about 3.1 million deaths in 2023 it kills more Indians than the next two causes combined.

plannedCharts.title: What kills babies (under 5)
plannedCharts.subtitle: IHME GBD 2023 · India · children under five, both sexes
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: For the youngest, death looks nothing like the all-ages chart. The biggest killers are the complications of birth itself, prematurity, asphyxia, infection, not chronic disease.

plannedCharts.title: What kills children (5 to 14)
plannedCharts.subtitle: IHME GBD 2023 · India · ages 5-14, both sexes
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: School-age children are mostly past the dangers of birth, so accidents move to the front: drowning is the single biggest killer, ahead of cancers and road crashes.

plannedCharts.title: What kills young adults (15 to 49)
plannedCharts.subtitle: IHME GBD 2023 · India · ages 15-49, both sexes
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: This is the chart the all-ages ranking hides. Among working-age Indians, road injuries and suicide are the third and fourth biggest killers, each around 1.5 lakh deaths, the tragedy of dying young.

plannedCharts.title: What kills the middle-aged (50 to 69)
plannedCharts.subtitle: IHME GBD 2023 · India · ages 50-69, both sexes
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: By middle age the non-communicable diseases take over completely. Cardiovascular disease alone kills over 1.2 million people aged 50-69, with cancer and chronic respiratory disease next.

plannedCharts.title: What kills the elderly (70 and older)
plannedCharts.subtitle: IHME GBD 2023 · India · ages 70+, both sexes
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: In old age, cardiovascular disease is overwhelming: nearly 1.6 million deaths among the over-70s in 2023, more than the next three causes combined, with chronic respiratory disease a distant second.

plannedCharts.title: How men and women die differently
plannedCharts.subtitle: SRS Cause of Death 2022-24 (Registrar General) · share of all male vs female deaths
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The biggest sex difference is injuries: 12.6% of male deaths versus 7.5% of female deaths, men die far more from accidents, suicide and violence. Women carry more 'ill-defined' deaths, a sign their causes go less often recorded.

plannedCharts.title: The rise of heart disease, 1980 to 2023
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Why the heart sits at the top, and how fast it got there. CVD deaths roughly tripled from under a million in 1980 to about 3.1 million in 2023 as the population grew, aged, and urbanised.

plannedCharts.title: The four non-communicable killers
plannedCharts.subtitle: IHME GBD 2023 · annual deaths, 1980 to 2023
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The non-communicable surge, broken into its four engines. Together these are the bulk of Indian deaths today, and all four are rising.

plannedCharts.title: What India's own death survey finds, 2022-24
plannedCharts.subtitle: SRS Cause of Death 2022-2024 (Registrar General) · nationally representative, verbal-autopsy
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: India's own nationally representative cause-of-death data, the strongest check on the modelled GBD estimates. It agrees on the headline, cardiovascular disease leads at 32.1% of all deaths, and unlike hospital records it counts the rural and at-home deaths most Indians actually have.

plannedCharts.title: The COVID scar, and the recovery
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: What the pandemic did to the death toll, and the important point the old data missed: it then receded. COVID deaths spiked in 2020-21 and fell back sharply by 2023.

plannedCharts.title: India's own death rate: spike, then recovery
plannedCharts.subtitle: SRS 2023 (Registrar General) · deaths per 1,000 population
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Are Indians dying at a lower rate? India's own Sample Registration System says yes, with a clear COVID interruption: the crude death rate jumped to 7.5 in 2021, then fell back to 6.4 by 2023.

plannedCharts.title: The retreat of infectious disease
plannedCharts.subtitle: IHME GBD 2023 · annual deaths, 1980 to 2023
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The other half of the story: as non-communicable deaths climb, infectious killers have fallen steeply. This is the epidemiological transition in one chart.

plannedCharts.title: Death by injury and suicide
plannedCharts.subtitle: IHME GBD 2023 · annual deaths, 1980 to 2023
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Injuries kill working-age Indians in their prime. Suicide and road crashes are the two largest, each around 200,000-250,000 deaths a year.

plannedCharts.title: Maternal mortality: the steepest fall
plannedCharts.subtitle: WHO/UN MMEIG · maternal deaths per 100,000 live births
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The single biggest public-health win on this page. Maternal deaths fell from 658 to about 80 per 100,000 live births, a nearly 90% decline.

plannedCharts.title: Fewer babies dying
plannedCharts.subtitle: SRS 2023 (Registrar General) · infant deaths per 1,000 live births
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Child survival is the engine behind India's falling overall death rate. The infant mortality rate fell to 25 per 1,000 live births in 2023.

plannedCharts.title: Total deaths each year
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The denominator behind everything else. Total annual deaths have risen even as the death rate has fallen, because the population is larger and older.

selectionRules: Start from the page question, not from the indicator name.
Choose the smallest set of visuals that explains scale, trend, structure, comparison, and caveat.
Prefer mobile-friendly charts: line charts with sparse ticks, horizontal bars, share strips, pyramids only when essential, and no dense tables.
Use one strong pull quote only when the evidence supports it with a number.
Add glossary blocks for technical terms that normal readers may not know.
caveats: Use only figures present in lockedNumbers or sourceSummaries.
If a related theme indicator is present, you may use it for context, but do not pretend it is the main answer.
If source coverage is thin, say so directly.
Do not compare states, districts, or cities unless the evidence packet contains those geographies.
forbiddenClaims: Do not infer causality from a trend unless the evidence explicitly supports it.
Do not cite a source that is not present in sourceSummaries.
